name: Sobriety Checkpoints
title: Sobriety Checkpoints
summary: Law enforcement stops vehicles at fixed locations to check drivers for alcohol or drug impairment. Checkpoints are highly visible and serve both as a direct enforcement tool and as a general deterrence measure. Consistent use reduces alcohol-involved crash rates by signaling to drivers that impaired driving will be detected.
effectivenessNote: Meta-analyses show 20% reduction in alcohol-involved crashes when used consistently.

name: High-Visibility Saturation Patrols
title: High-Visibility Saturation Patrols
summary: Concentrated law enforcement patrols in high-risk areas and time windows with publicized enforcement messaging. Most effective when paired with earned media campaigns that make the public aware that extra patrols are operating.
effectivenessNote: Reduces alcohol-related crashes, particularly when combined with media outreach.

name: DUI Courts / Drug Courts
title: DUI Courts / Drug Courts
summary: Specialized court programs combining judicial oversight with treatment and supervision for repeat DUI offenders. Participants receive close monitoring including frequent alcohol testing, court appearances, and treatment services as an alternative to incarceration.
effectivenessNote: Reduces recidivism among repeat DUI offenders by 10–20% compared to traditional sentencing.

name: Ignition Interlocks
title: Ignition Interlocks
summary: Breath-test devices installed in vehicles that prevent the engine from starting if the driver's BAC exceeds a set threshold. Most effective when required for all DUI offenders including first offenders, with monitoring and compliance requirements.
effectivenessNote: Reduces repeat DUI offenses by 60–90% while installed; all-offender laws more effective than first-offender exemptions.

name: Administrative License Revocation (ALR)
title: Administrative License Revocation (ALR)
summary: Immediate administrative suspension of a driver's license upon arrest for DUI or refusal to submit to a BAC test, enacted separately from criminal proceedings. The swift and certain penalty serves as a deterrent independent of criminal court outcomes.
effectivenessNote: States with ALR laws have 5–9% lower alcohol-involved fatal crash rates than states without.

name: Automated Speed Enforcement (Speed Cameras)
title: Automated Speed Enforcement (Speed Cameras)
summary: Camera systems that automatically detect and issue citations to speeding drivers, typically deployed in work zones, school zones, or high-risk corridors. Most effective when combined with public awareness campaigns and graduated penalty structures.
effectivenessNote: Reduces speeds by 10–15 mph and reduces speeding-related crashes by 20–40% at deployment sites.

name: High-Visibility Speed Enforcement
title: High-Visibility Speed Enforcement
summary: Concentrated, well-publicized police speed enforcement campaigns using earned media to maximize deterrence effect. The combination of actual enforcement with public knowledge of the campaign produces greater speed reductions than enforcement alone.
effectivenessNote: Publicized campaigns reduce mean speeds and speed-related crashes during and after the campaign period.

name: Road Diet (Lane Reduction)
title: Road Diet (Lane Reduction)
summary: Reconfiguring a roadway to reduce the number of through lanes, often replacing them with a center turn lane and/or bike lanes. Reduces vehicle speeds, shortens pedestrian crossing distances, and reduces conflict points.
effectivenessNote: Reduces total crashes by 19–47% and vehicle speeds by 2–5 mph on converted corridors.

name: Roundabouts
title: Roundabouts
summary: Replacing signalized or stop-controlled intersections with roundabouts eliminates high-speed right-angle and head-on conflicts. The geometry forces speed reduction and removes the stop-and-go acceleration that leads to speeding between intersections.
effectivenessNote: Reduces fatal and injury crashes by 72–90% compared to conventional intersections.

name: Handheld Cell Phone Use Ban
title: Handheld Cell Phone Use Ban
summary: Laws prohibiting drivers from holding a phone while driving. Primary enforcement (can be cited without another violation) is significantly more effective than secondary enforcement. All-driver bans produce stronger effects than teen-only bans.
effectivenessNote: Primary enforcement bans reduce handheld phone use by 50–70% and phone-related crashes by 10–15%.

name: Texting While Driving Ban
title: Texting While Driving Ban
summary: Laws specifically prohibiting composing, reading, or sending electronic messages while driving. Most effective with primary enforcement authority and meaningful penalties. Often paired with high-visibility enforcement campaigns.
effectivenessNote: Reduces texting-while-driving prevalence by 30–50% when actively enforced.

name: High-Visibility Distracted Driving Enforcement
title: High-Visibility Distracted Driving Enforcement
summary: Concentrated enforcement campaigns targeting phone use and other distracted driving behaviors, paired with public communications. The 'U Drive. U Text. U Pay.' model has demonstrated effectiveness in reducing observed phone use.
effectivenessNote: Campaign periods show 10–20% reductions in observed handheld phone use.

name: Primary Seat Belt Enforcement Law
title: Primary Seat Belt Enforcement Law
summary: Laws allowing officers to stop and cite a driver or passenger solely for not wearing a seat belt, without needing another traffic violation. Primary laws are consistently more effective than secondary enforcement laws at increasing belt use and reducing unrestrained occupant fatalities.
effectivenessNote: Primary enforcement laws increase belt use by 10–15 percentage points and reduce unrestrained occupant fatalities by 8–9%.
chapter: Chapter 2: Seat Belts and Child Restraints

name: High-Visibility Belt Enforcement (Click It or Ticket)
title: High-Visibility Belt Enforcement (Click It or Ticket)
summary: Nationally coordinated, high-visibility enforcement mobilization combining intensive law enforcement with paid media, earned media, and social media campaigns. The 'Click It or Ticket' model is the most extensively evaluated belt use program in the US.
effectivenessNote: Increases belt use by 3–10 percentage points; effects persist after the campaign period.
chapter: Chapter 2: Seat Belts and Child Restraints

name: Seat Belt Community Outreach Programs
title: Seat Belt Community Outreach Programs
summary: Targeted programs reaching populations with lower belt use rates (e.g., rural communities, pickup truck drivers, commercial vehicle operators). Programs combine education, incentives, and peer-to-peer messaging tailored to specific demographic contexts.
effectivenessNote: Community-level programs increase belt use 5–15% among targeted populations.
chapter: Chapter 2: Seat Belts and Child Restraints

name: Crosswalk Visibility and Safety Improvements
title: Crosswalk Visibility and Safety Improvements
summary: Engineering improvements to crosswalk design including high-visibility markings, pedestrian refuge islands, curb extensions (bulb-outs), raised crosswalks, and improved lighting. Most effective at unsignalized locations and on higher-speed roads.
effectivenessNote: High-visibility crosswalk markings reduce pedestrian crashes at marked crossings by 25–50%.

name: Leading Pedestrian Intervals (LPI)
title: Leading Pedestrian Intervals (LPI)
summary: Signal timing change that gives pedestrians a 3–7 second head start before vehicles receive a green light, increasing pedestrian visibility to turning drivers. Low cost, easily implemented at existing signalized intersections.
effectivenessNote: Reduces pedestrian-vehicle conflicts at intersections by 60% and pedestrian crashes by 13%.

name: Safe Routes and Pedestrian Safety Plans
title: Safe Routes and Pedestrian Safety Plans
summary: Systematic planning processes that identify high-risk pedestrian corridors and prioritize infrastructure investments. Includes Safe Routes to School programs, pedestrian safety action plans, and Vision Zero corridors targeting the highest-injury networks.
effectivenessNote: Corridor-level interventions reduce pedestrian crashes 15–35% on targeted networks.

name: Hours of Service Regulations (Commercial Drivers)
title: Hours of Service Regulations (Commercial Drivers)
summary: Federal regulations limiting driving hours and requiring rest periods for commercial motor vehicle operators. Compliance monitoring and enforcement are key to effectiveness. Applicable to interstate commercial carriers.
effectivenessNote: HOS regulations reduce commercial vehicle drowsy driving crashes; effectiveness depends on enforcement compliance.

name: Drowsy Driving Awareness and Education Campaigns
title: Drowsy Driving Awareness and Education Campaigns
summary: Public awareness programs targeting high-risk groups (young drivers, shift workers, commercial drivers) with messaging about the dangers of drowsy driving and strategies to prevent it. Most effective when paired with clear behavioral guidance (pull over, take a break, use a passenger).
effectivenessNote: Limited direct evidence on crash reduction; increases awareness and self-reported behavior change.

name: Wrong-Way Driver Detection Systems
title: Wrong-Way Driver Detection Systems
summary: Sensor-based systems at freeway ramps that detect wrong-way vehicles and trigger alerts to drivers (flashing lights, warning signs) and notifications to law enforcement dispatch. Most effective on high-risk divided highway access points.
effectivenessNote: Detection systems alert wrong-way drivers in time to self-correct in majority of activations; crash data still emerging.

name: Wrong-Way Signing and Pavement Markings
title: Wrong-Way Signing and Pavement Markings
summary: Enhanced 'Wrong Way' and 'Do Not Enter' signing with larger signs, retroreflective materials, flashing beacons, and red pavement markings at freeway entrance ramps. Low-cost countermeasure appropriate for all divided highway access points.
effectivenessNote: Enhanced signing packages reduce wrong-way entries by 30–40% at treated ramps.

name: Hit-and-Run Reward and Reporting Programs
title: Hit-and-Run Reward and Reporting Programs
summary: Programs offering financial rewards for information leading to identification of hit-and-run drivers, combined with public awareness campaigns about reporting requirements. Often managed through Crime Stoppers or similar platforms.
effectivenessNote: Increases tip volume and case clearance rates; limited crash reduction evidence.
chapter: OBSR: Aggressive and Evasive Driving Behavioral Research

name: Edge Line and Centerline Rumble Strips
title: Edge Line and Centerline Rumble Strips
summary: Milled or raised patterns on road shoulders and centerlines produce audible and tactile alerts when a vehicle departs its lane, warning drowsy or inattentive drivers before a run-off-road or head-on crash occurs. Among the most cost-effective infrastructure countermeasures available, eligible for Highway Safety Improvement Program funding.
effectivenessNote: Shoulder rumble strips reduce run-off-road fatal crashes by 25–30%. Centerline rumble strips reduce head-on and opposite-direction sideswipe crashes by 14–18%.
chapter: Uniform Guidelines: Lane Departure / Drowsy Driving Program Area
sourceDocument: Uniform Guidelines for State Highway Safety Programs, NHTSA

name: HAWK Pedestrian Hybrid Beacons
title: HAWK Pedestrian Hybrid Beacons
summary: High-intensity Activated crossWalK (HAWK) beacons are pedestrian-activated signals installed on high-speed arterials where signalized intersections are not warranted. When a pedestrian pushes the button, the beacon cycles through yellow, red, and flashing red phases — stopping vehicle traffic while pedestrians cross — then returns to dark when clear. Designated FHWA Proven Safety Countermeasure.
effectivenessNote: Reduces total pedestrian crashes by 69% and pedestrian fatalities by 29% at installations compared to uncontrolled crossings.
chapter: Uniform Guidelines: Pedestrian and Bicycle Safety Program Area
sourceDocument: Uniform Guidelines for State Highway Safety Programs, NHTSA

name: Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) Systems
title: Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) Systems
summary: Three-stage licensing systems for teen drivers (learner permit, intermediate license, full license) that progressively expose young drivers to independent driving while restricting nighttime driving, number of teen passengers, and mobile device use. Young drivers (16–20) are disproportionately involved in speeding-related fatalities; GDL systems are the most evidence-supported young driver intervention.
effectivenessNote: Comprehensive GDL systems reduce fatal crash involvement for 16-year-old drivers by 26–41%. Passenger restrictions alone reduce teen passenger fatality risk by 15%.
chapter: Uniform Guidelines: Young Driver Safety Program Area
sourceDocument: Uniform Guidelines for State Highway Safety Programs, NHTSA

name: Standard Field Sobriety Testing (SFST) Officer Training
title: Standard Field Sobriety Testing (SFST) Officer Training
summary: NHTSA-standardized training program certifying law enforcement officers in roadside alcohol impairment detection using three validated tests: Horizontal Gaze Nystagmus (HGN), Walk and Turn, and One Leg Stand. SFST certification is a foundational requirement for officers conducting DUI enforcement and a condition for Section 405d grant eligibility.
effectivenessNote: Officers trained in SFST are significantly more accurate in detecting alcohol impairment at or above 0.08 BAC than untrained officers; supports DUI arrest quality and court admissibility.
chapter: Uniform Guidelines: Impaired Driving Program Area
sourceDocument: Uniform Guidelines for State Highway Safety Programs, NHTSA

name: Nighttime Seat Belt Enforcement Mobilizations
title: Nighttime Seat Belt Enforcement Mobilizations
summary: Concentrated enforcement campaigns specifically targeting nighttime seat belt non-use, which is two to three times higher than daytime rates. Unrestrained nighttime drivers account for a disproportionate share of fatal occupant ejection crashes. These campaigns pair enforcement with media messaging targeted at high-risk demographic groups (e.g., young male rural drivers).
effectivenessNote: Nighttime enforcement mobilizations increase belt use among target populations by 8–15 percentage points; reduce unrestrained occupant fatalities at night by 10–20%.
chapter: Uniform Guidelines: Occupant Protection Program Area
sourceDocument: Uniform Guidelines for State Highway Safety Programs, NHTSA

name: Ridesharing and Sober Ride Programs
title: Ridesharing and Sober Ride Programs
summary: Programs that partner with transportation network companies (TNCs), taxi services, or dedicated sober-ride programs to provide subsidized or promoted alternatives to drunk driving. Most effective when activated around high-risk periods (holidays, major sporting events) and when promoted alongside enforcement messaging. NHTSA behavioral research shows these programs complement law enforcement strategies.
effectivenessNote: TNC availability is associated with 7–25% reductions in drunk driving fatalities in urban markets. Dedicated sober-ride programs show increased usage when incentives (free/discounted rides) are combined with earned media.

name: Teen and Young Adult Distracted Driving Programs
title: Teen and Young Adult Distracted Driving Programs
summary: Peer-to-peer education and engagement programs targeting 16–24 year old drivers, who have the highest rates of cell phone use while driving. Research-tested programs use social norming, peer educators, and social media amplification. Effective approaches involve school-based delivery, student ambassador models, and linkage to GDL enforcement.
effectivenessNote: Peer-led programs reduce self-reported texting while driving by 15–25% among teen participants; most effective when paired with school administrative policies.

name: Roadway Lighting at High-Risk Pedestrian Locations
title: Roadway Lighting at High-Risk Pedestrian Locations
summary: Targeted installation or upgrade of roadway and intersection lighting at high-pedestrian-crash locations. NHTSA behavioral research shows that more than 75% of pedestrian fatalities occur in dark conditions — disproportionately at non-intersection locations on high-speed arterials. Lighting is a cost-effective countermeasure when targeted using crash data.
effectivenessNote: Roadway lighting reduces nighttime pedestrian crashes by 30–50% at treated locations. Combined with crosswalk treatments, reductions exceed 60% in some corridor studies.

name: Hit-and-Run Penalty Enhancement and Public Awareness
title: Hit-and-Run Penalty Enhancement and Public Awareness
summary: Legislative and public education strategies that increase the criminal penalties for hit-and-run crashes (particularly those involving injury or death) and communicate consequences to the public. Research indicates that many hit-and-run crashes involve drivers who are impaired and flee to avoid DUI detection. Combined messaging about legal exposure for fleeing vs. remaining at the scene may change driver decision calculus.
effectivenessNote: Penalty enhancements show modest deterrence effects in pre/post studies; most effective when combined with high-profile prosecutions and earned media coverage.
chapter: OBSR: Aggressive and Evasive Driving Behavioral Research

name: Employer-Based Commercial Driver Fatigue Management Programs
title: Employer-Based Commercial Driver Fatigue Management Programs
summary: Structured workplace programs for transportation employers (trucking, transit, delivery) that implement fatigue risk management systems including scheduling controls, driver fatigue education, sleep disorder screening, and compliance monitoring. NHTSA behavioral research identifies commercial drivers and shift workers as the highest-risk populations for drowsy-driving involvement in fatal crashes.
effectivenessNote: Fatigue risk management systems reduce drowsy driving events among commercial fleets by 15–30% in fleet-level implementations; most effective with sleep disorder screening component.
chapter: OBSR: Drowsy and Distracted Driving Behavioral Research
